name: Reagan et al. Six Basic Emotional Arcs (Computational Story Lab)
core_claim: Reagan, Mitchell, Kiley, Danforth & Dodds (2016, 'The emotional arcs of stories are dominated by six basic shapes', EPJ Data Science) claim that the emotional trajectory of a story — the rise and fall of sentiment (happiness/valence) measured continuously across the text from beginning to end — is a fundamental, empirically recoverable dimension of narrative, and that the space of these trajectories is overwhelmingly dominated by a small number (six) of basic shapes. The central claim is not about what a story is ABOUT (topic, theme, character, moral) but about the SHAPE of its emotional time series: whether sentiment trends up, down, falls-then-rises, rises-then-falls, or oscillates in a characteristic way. By extracting a windowed valence signal from 1,327 English-language works of fiction from Project Gutenberg and applying three independent methods (singular value decomposition / matrix factorisation, hierarchical clustering, and an unsupervised self-organising map / neural approach), they argue the dominant modes reduce to six recurring arcs: 'Rags to riches' (steady emotional rise), 'Tragedy' or 'Riches to rags' (steady fall), 'Man in a hole' (fall then rise), 'Icarus' (rise then fall), 'Cinderella' (rise–fall–rise), and 'Oedipus' (fall–rise–fall). The further, weaker claim is that arc shape relates to reception: certain arcs (e.g. 'Man in a hole', 'Oedipus', and the 'Cinderella' double-rise) were associated with greater download counts on Gutenberg, a proxy for popularity. The framework is the empirical, data-driven counterpart to Vonnegut's informal 'shapes of stories' lecture, which it explicitly operationalises.
descriptive_vs_experiential: This framework occupies a distinctive level that is neither topical-descriptive (what the story is about) nor directly experiential-affective in the psychological sense (what the viewer feels). It is best described as STRUCTURAL-AFFECTIVE: it captures the shape of the emotional valence signal of the WORK over its duration. It is a property of the text's sentiment dynamics, not of viewer experience and not of subject matter. It is adjacent to the experiential dimension — the arc describes the affective contour the story PRESENTS — but it is measured as an objective feature of the artefact (a sentiment time series), not as a self-reported feeling state. So it is a structural representation of affect: the trajectory and turning-points of emotional valence, abstracted away from genre, theme, and the specific events that cause the rises and falls. Two stories with identical arc shapes can be about completely different things; two thematically identical stories can have opposite arcs.
key_dimensions: The primary dimension is the EMOTIONAL ARC: a one-dimensional valence-over-narrative-time signal, typically normalised to a fixed length and mean-centred. Within that, the concrete categories proposed are the six basic arc shapes, each describable by the sign/sequence of its major movements: (1) 'Rags to riches' — monotonic RISE; (2) 'Tragedy' / 'Riches to rags' — monotonic FALL; (3) 'Man in a hole' — FALL then RISE (down-up); (4) 'Icarus' — RISE then FALL (up-down); (5) 'Cinderella' — RISE–FALL–RISE; (6) 'Oedipus' — FALL–RISE–FALL. Underlying these are continuous quantitative dimensions the method actually measures: the SVD/PCA modes (the dominant eigen-shapes of the trajectory space, of which the six arcs are combinations and the first few modes explain most variance), the number and direction of major emotional turning-points (roughly 0, 1, or 2 inflections), overall valence trend (net up vs net down), and emotional VOLATILITY/amplitude (how much sentiment swings). These continuous quantities — net direction, number of reversals, swing amplitude — are the candidate axes for a controlled vocabulary, arguably more useful than the six named labels themselves.
cross_cultural_validity: Weak / largely untested cross-culturally as published. The corpus is explicitly 1,327 ENGLISH-LANGUAGE works of fiction from Project Gutenberg, skewed toward pre-1925 public-domain Western (largely Anglo-American and European) literature, scored with English sentiment lexicons — principally the labMT 'hedonometer' word-happiness list, which is English and derived from English-language word ratings. So both the stimuli and the measurement instrument are Western/English-centric. The CONSTRUCT (sentiment rises and falls over a narrative) is plausibly universal — stories everywhere have emotional ups and downs — and the hedonometer team has built happiness lexicons in multiple languages, suggesting the method ports in principle. But the specific finding that exactly SIX shapes dominate, and their relative frequencies and popularity associations, has not been robustly demonstrated on non-Western, non-English, or contemporary screen narratives. What is likely to break down: (a) lexicon coverage and valence calibration across languages; (b) culturally specific narrative conventions (e.g. East Asian kishotenketsu structures with a non-conflict 'twist', or episodic/cyclical forms) that may not reduce to the same up/down primitives; (c) the corpus's literary-historical bias means the arc frequencies reflect a particular canon, not human storytelling in general. [uncertain]
empirical_grounding: This is the framework's signature strength and the explicit reason it was selected: it is COMPUTATIONAL and DATA-DRIVEN, not armchair-structuralist. The six shapes were not assumed — they emerged from three methodologically independent analyses (SVD/matrix factorisation, hierarchical clustering, and an unsupervised neural/self-organising-map approach) converging on the same low-dimensional set, which is a genuine triangulation of the empirical claim. The arcs are derived from measured sentiment signals over real texts, giving them stronger grounding than Propp, Booker, or Campbell, which are interpretive and lack quantitative validation. The reception link is CORRELATIONAL and proxy-based: arc shape was associated with Project Gutenberg DOWNLOAD counts (a popularity proxy), not with measured enjoyment, completion, or preference — so the 'which arcs succeed' claim is the weakest, confounded part (downloads conflate age, canon status, school curricula, and visibility, not just emotional appeal). Important caveats on quality: sentiment is measured by a bag-of-words happiness lexicon, which captures word-level valence but misses irony, negation, and dramatic context; smoothing/windowing choices affect the recovered shapes; and 'six' is partly a representational choice (how many modes you keep), so the discreteness should be read as 'a few dominant continuous modes' rather than a hard taxonomy. To validate LLM-extracted dimensions against this framework one would compute the hedonometer arc for a corpus of screen synopses or scripts and check whether an LLM's predicted arc shape (or net-direction / reversal-count / volatility estimate) matches the lexicon-derived signal — and, separately and more valuably, whether either predicts real viewer enjoyment/completion data.
operationalisability: Moderately operationalisable from synopsis, with a clear caveat about extraction unit. The ORIGINAL method needs the full running text (a windowed word-level sentiment signal), which a short synopsis does NOT provide — a synopsis is too compressed to yield a reliable per-window valence time series. However, the OUTPUT abstraction (arc shape) is exactly the kind of thing an LLM can estimate from a synopsis when the synopsis describes the emotional trajectory ('a struggling boxer rises to glory' → Rags-to-riches; 'a happy family is destroyed' → Tragedy; 'a detective hits rock bottom then solves the case' → Man-in-a-hole). The natural extraction unit is the WHOLE WORK for a film, and per-SEASON or per-ARC for serialised TV (a season often has its own emotional contour distinct from the series). Recommended LLM encoding: extract coarse, robust quantities — net emotional direction (ends-happier / ends-sadder / flat), number of major reversals (0/1/2+), and which named arc is the closest fit — rather than a fake continuous valence curve. Failure modes: (1) synopses describe SETUP not RESOLUTION, so endings (which determine arc direction) are often hidden or spoiler-suppressed in marketing copy — the single biggest failure; (2) irony, tonal ambiguity, and ambiguous/open endings resist a single shape; (3) ensemble or multi-plot works have several superimposed arcs that don't reduce to one shape; (4) LLM may infer the GENRE-expected arc rather than the actual one (assuming every underdog story is rags-to-riches); (5) comedies and tragicomedies with rapid oscillation get flattened; (6) episodic/procedural TV may have no series-level arc at all. So: arc DIRECTION and reversal-count are reasonably extractable when the ending is known; the fine-grained six-way label and any precise curve are not reliably extractable from synopsis alone.
motivation_served: Arc shape is a coarse but real proxy for HEDONIC vs EUDAIMONIC payoff and for mood outcome. Upward-ending arcs (Rags-to-riches, Man-in-a-hole, Cinderella) tend to serve hedonic enjoyment and positive MOOD REGULATION — the satisfying, uplifting, 'feel-good' resolution — making them safe mood-repair picks. Downward-ending arcs (Tragedy, Icarus, Oedipus) lean eudaimonic: being moved, catharsis, meaningful sadness, the appreciation of loss — chosen when viewers want to feel something profound rather than feel good. The number of reversals also maps to engagement intensity: more turning-points (Cinderella, Oedipus) means a more dramatic emotional ride. For GROUP viewing this is unusually useful because arc shape is a shareable, low-dimensional handle on the EXPERIENCE two people will jointly have: a couple agreeing they want 'something uplifting tonight' (ends-happier) versus 'something that wrecks us' (ends-sadder) is essentially negotiating arc direction. It is a clean axis to trade off on — one person's tolerance for a downbeat ending versus another's need for catharsis — and far easier for two people to align on than fine-grained themes. The arc gives a concrete 'emotional destination' both parties can consent to before pressing play.
receiver_dependence: Largely WORK-SIDE / ABSOLUTE, which is its key advantage and a useful contrast to receiver-dependent frameworks. The emotional arc is, by construction, a measured property of the text alone — the sentiment time series exists independent of any viewer, like gore level rather than like 'whether the values resonate'. The arc SHAPE and its turning points are absolute features of the work. What is RELATIONAL is the second-order question of how a given viewer RESPONDS to a given shape: some people seek upbeat endings and avoid tragedy, others actively prefer sad/eudaimonic arcs (sad-film paradox); tolerance for downbeat or ambiguous endings is a stable individual difference. So the framework cleanly separates into a work-side, synopsis/text-estimable layer (the arc itself) and a user-side preference layer (this user's affinity for ends-happier vs ends-sadder, for high vs low emotional volatility). For a two-person recommender this is ideal: store the arc as an absolute work attribute, and model each viewer's arc-preference separately, then the joint problem becomes matching a single work-side arc against two user-side preference profiles — a tractable trade-off because the arc value doesn't change between viewers, only its desirability does.
blind_spots: (1) It is ONLY valence-over-time — a single affective dimension — so it collapses very different stories with the same shape; it cannot tell rags-to-riches-via-war from rags-to-riches-via-romance, and says nothing about theme, character, morality, or meaning. (2) It ignores AROUSAL and discrete emotions (fear vs sadness vs anger all read as 'low valence'); a horror film and a tearjerker can share an arc. (3) Bag-of-words sentiment misses IRONY, sarcasm, negation, and contextual reversal — the very things sophisticated narratives run on. (4) Endings dominate the shape, but many works are valued for ambiguity, openness, or refusing emotional resolution — these resist a clean shape. (5) The popularity finding is download-based and confounded; it does not establish that any arc is more ENJOYED. (6) It is built on long-form prose fiction; transfer to film, episodic/serial TV, anime, and especially plotless/vibe-driven or procedural works is unvalidated — many great works (slice-of-life, mood pieces, ensemble mosaics) may have flat or incoherent arcs yet high appeal. (7) 'Six' is partly an artefact of how many modes you retain; the real object is a continuous low-dimensional space, and forcing discrete labels loses information. (8) It cannot capture pacing, momentum, suspense, or comedic rhythm — only the slow emotional drift. (9) Co-viewing, social, and re-watch dynamics are entirely outside its scope.
vocabulary_implications: Arc shape should NOT be added as topical theme slugs; it belongs in a separate EMOTIONAL-TRAJECTORY facet, parallel to (not mixed with) the existing topical/moral/identity themes — similar to how transportation's immersion axis was kept separate. Recommended encoding: a small controlled facet the LLM estimates from synopsis where the ending is known. Proposed structured fields rather than six opaque labels: 'emotional-direction' {ends-uplifting / ends-downbeat / flat-or-ambiguous}, 'emotional-reversals' {0 / 1 / 2-plus}, and 'emotional-volatility' {low / medium / high}. Optionally a derived nearest-arc label from those primitives: rags-to-riches (up, 0 reversals), tragedy (down, 0), man-in-a-hole (up, 1, dips first), icarus (down, 1, peaks first), cinderella (up, 2), oedipus (down, 2). Synopsis-estimable SLUGS that could enter the texture/structure facet: 'feel-good-uplift', 'downbeat-ending', 'redemptive-recovery' (man-in-a-hole), 'tragic-fall' (icarus/oedipus), 'emotional-rollercoaster' (high reversals/volatility). CRITICAL flags on extractability: the arc depends on the ENDING, which marketing synopses often hide — so 'emotional-direction' should be marked low-confidence whenever the synopsis is spoiler-suppressed, and ideally backstopped by other signals (reviews, audience tags like 'sad ending', or actual hedonometer scoring of scripts/subtitles where available). The fine-grained six-way label and any continuous valence curve are NOT reliably extractable from synopsis and should not be stored as if certain.
relationship_to_existing_vocabulary: This framework supplies the AFFECTIVE-CONTOUR axis the current vocabulary lacks and partly overlaps with several existing structure/arc slugs. It directly VALIDATES and sharpens: 'underdog-rise' and 'redemption-arc' (rags-to-riches / man-in-a-hole), 'rise-and-fall' (icarus/oedipus — an almost exact match to the up-down and fall-rise-fall arcs), 'guilt-and-atonement' and 'betrayal-and-forgiveness' (often man-in-a-hole recovery shapes), and 'grief-and-loss', 'meditation-on-death', 'war-and-its-toll' (tragedy/downbeat arcs). It clarifies that several of these existing slugs are conflating TOPIC with TRAJECTORY: 'rise-and-fall' is really an arc-shape descriptor masquerading as a theme, and 'underdog-rise' bundles a topical situation (underdog) with an emotional direction (rise) — the framework suggests splitting the direction out into the new emotional-trajectory facet so a downbeat underdog story (underdog who loses) is representable. The GAPS it reveals: HelpME2C has no explicit, work-side handle on emotional DIRECTION (ends-up vs ends-down), on number of emotional REVERSALS, or on VOLATILITY — yet 'how does it leave you feeling' and 'is the ending uplifting or devastating' are among the most decision-relevant questions for a couple choosing what to watch together. Adding the emotional-trajectory facet fills that hole and, because the arc is an absolute work property, it slots cleanly into the work-side layer while a separate user-side 'arc preference' captures who likes which ending.
key_sources: Reagan, A. J., Mitchell, L., Kiley, D., Danforth, C. M., & Dodds, P. S. (2016). The emotional arcs of stories are dominated by six basic shapes. EPJ Data Science, 5:31 — the primary paper, OPEN ACCESS: https://epjdatascience.springeropen.com/articles/10.1140/epjds/s13688-016-0093-1 . Preprint (free): arXiv:1606.07772, https://arxiv.org/abs/1606.07772 . Author copy PDF (free): https://cdanfort.w3.uvm.edu/research/2016-reagan-epj.pdf . Project page (free): https://pdodds.w3.uvm.edu/research/papers/reagan2016c/ . Related infrastructure: the hedonometer / labMT happiness lexicon, http://hedonometer.org (free) — the sentiment instrument underlying the method (Dodds et al., 2011, PLoS ONE, open access). Background: Vonnegut's informal 'shapes of stories' lecture, which the paper operationalises (widely available, free). Popular summaries (free): Aeon Ideas, https://aeon.co/ideas/when-the-stories-add-up-the-six-narrative-arcs-in-fiction ; EurekAlert release, https://www.eurekalert.org/news-releases/510292 . The 1,327-book filtered Project Gutenberg corpus and code are described in the paper and partially available via the authors' Computational Story Lab repositories (free).
